{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city closeup.jpg |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sumiyoshi Jinja (Sumiyoshi Shrine) | image = [[File:Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city closeup.jpg|250px]] | caption = Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja Shrine in Kakuda City | map_type = Japan Miyagi | coordinates = {{Coord|37|56|28.6|N|140|47|53.1|E|region:JP-04_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sumiyoshi Jinja}} | location = Eda-no Aza Kōriyama 5, {{ill|Kakuda, Miyagi|ja|角田市|lt=Kakuda City|12=draft}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|12=draft}} | deity = {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|ja|住吉三神|lt=The Three Male Deities of Sumiyoshi|12=draft}} | founded = {{ill|Kareki era|ja|嘉暦元年|lt=Kareki 1 (1326)|12=draft}} | type = Former Village Shrine (Sonsha) | honden = Ichimonsha nagare-zukuri | other_name = Kareki Myōjin, Kareki Myōjin (枯木明神) | festival = {{ill|April 29|ja|4月29日|12=draft}} }}
'''Sumiyoshi Jinja''' (住吉神社, Sumiyoshi Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Kakuda, Miyagi|ja|角田市|lt=Kakuda City|12=draft}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|12=draft}}, Japan. Under the old shrine ranking system, it was a village shrine (sonsha).
== Enshrined Deities == * The shrine enshrines the three male deities of {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|ja|住吉三神|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|12=draft}}: ** Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto (Other name: Iwatsuchi no Kami, Kamichi no O no Kami) ** Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto (Other name: Akatsuchi no Kami, Nakatsuchi no O no Kami) ** Sokotsutsuo no Mikoto (Other name: Sokotsuchi no Kami)
== History == The site where Sumiyoshi Jinja stands is part of the “Kōriyama Site,” believed to have been the location of the administrative center (gun-ga) of Igu District and a temple complex. In a Heisei 17 (2005) excavation of the Kōriyama Site, a talc magatama with a “child-carrying” shape was found in a field adjacent to Sumiyoshi Jinja, indicating that from the early Heian period (8th–9th century) onward, rituals and prayers were likely conducted near the shrine’s current location.
Originally, the shrine was revered under the name “Sumiyoshiyama Iō-ji” during the period of Shinbutsu Konkō (Shinto-Buddhist syncretism). At that time, Sumiyoshiyama Iō-ji enshrined both the Sumiyoshi Sanjin and Yakushi Nyorai as the honji-butsu (Buddhist counterpart of the Shinto deity), serving as a place of healing, warding off illness, and bringing stability to the populace.
On the 29th day of the 3rd month of Kareki 1 (1326), during the reign of Emperor Go-Daigo in the Kamakura period, the divine spirits of the Sumiyoshi Deities were invited (kanjō) from the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|12=draft}} in Settsu Province (present-day Osaka City). This allowed the official use of the name “Sumiyoshi Jinja,” but local people referred to it as “Kareki Myōjin” or “Kareki Myōjin” after the Kareki era. Records indicate that at that time, the shrine enshrined Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto and Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto. During the Kareki era, the Kōriyama area was part of “Omi-gō,” one of the five hamlets of Igu District, and people traveling towards Mutsu Province likely passed near the shrine.
According to the “An’ei Fudoki” (published in An’ei 8 [1780]), around Genroku 14 (1702), a Shugendō practitioner of Onyozan Tōkō-in of Fujio District (now a defunct temple) named Fudō-in Genma invited the Sumiyoshiyama Iō-ji to become a branch temple of Tōkō-in under the name “Honzan-ha Zenkō-in.” Zenkō-in and Sumiyoshi-sha (the shrine) served as a bettōsho (sub-temple or attached temple). Later, Zenkō-in and Sumiyoshi-sha were destroyed by fire, losing all temple-shrine assets and old shrine buildings, leaving no historical records. Under the third head priest Zenkō-in Shinkyō (who died in Meiwa 5 [1768]), the shrine buildings were rebuilt in Shōtoku 2 (1712), and the name was changed to “Sumiyoshi Jinja,” becoming the tutelary shrine (chinju) of the village.
During a 1979 (Shōwa 54) excavation at the Kōriyama Site, remains of buildings facing south with supporting pillars and lotus-patterned round eave tiles (fukuben renge-mon kawara) were found. These discoveries indicated the presence of a fully tiled roof structure in the past, suggesting the prosperity and reverence toward Sumiyoshi Jinja at that time. The family that served as shrine priests, the Onashi family, has a series of ancestral graves bearing titles such as Gon Sōzu and Hōin, indicating they served as Hōin of Zenkō-in (or bettō of Sumiyoshi Jinja) for generations.
In Meiji 13 (1880) August, the Kakuda Prefectural General Affairs Bureau issued an order for the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. Onashi Zenkō, the son of the then Hōin Kenryō, became the shrine’s “shashō” (equivalent to a modern-day chief priest) of Sumiyoshi Jinja in Meiji 15 (1882). Meanwhile, Yakushi-ji, the bettōsho in Hatanaka of the Eda-no area, was separated and headed by Zenkō’s younger brother Kenkaku as Hōin. From this time on, Sumiyoshi Jinja completely separated from the temple. In Meiji 5 (1872) October, Sumiyoshi Jinja was listed as a village shrine, and in Meiji 40 (1907) January, it was designated as a Heihaku Kyōshin Jinja (a shrine receiving offerings from the state).
Onashi Zenkō died in Taishō 3 (1914), and his adopted son Onashi Yasunori became the shashō. Onashi Yasunori served as the principal of Edano Elementary School from Meiji 29 to Taishō 4 (1896-1915). After retiring, he contributed as the shrine priest of Sumiyoshi Jinja, and in Shōwa 3 (1928) he was instrumental in building the current shrine buildings. After his death, there was no resident priest, and the shrine was looked after by priests who also served Enkihidakahiko Jinja in Shimada and Kosai Kashima Jinja in Marumori Town’s Kozai area. In Shōwa 28 (1953), the granddaughter-in-law of Onashi Yasunori became the chief priest (gūji), and from that time to the present, the Onashi family has again served as the shrine’s priestly family.
== Precincts == * First and Second Torii Gates ** The area around the first torii gate is farmland, where cloth-impressed pottery shards believed to be from Igu District’s ancient government center (gun-ga) have been excavated. ** Along the approach, there is a small stone shrine (sekishin) dedicated to “Dōso-Ōkami and Ushitora-Inai Myōjin.”
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:First torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|First torii gate of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Douso-ookami and Ushitora-Inai-myoujin shrine.JPG|Stone shrine for Dōso-Ōkami and Ushitora-Inai Myōjin </gallery>
* Shrine buildings (Haiden, Heiden, and Honden in Ichimonsha Nagare-zukuri style) ** The foundation stones (soshiban) of the shrine buildings are partially reused from the Igu District gun-ga site.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|Sumiyoshi Jinja precincts File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Sumiyoshi Jinja precincts File:Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 1.JPG|Haiden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Carving of Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine.JPG|Carving on the Haiden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Honden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|Honden of Sumiyoshi Jinja </gallery>
* Auxiliary Shrines * Stone monument for “Mimachi” (Perpetual Serpent Vigil) ** Mimachi is the festival day for Benzaiten on an auspicious “Kishi (tsuchinoto-mi)” day, venerating the serpent deity. The serpent, as the messenger of Benzaiten, protects silkworm cultivation by controlling mice that harm silkworms, thus revered as a guardian of sericulture. * Cat deity Stone Monument ** Like the Mimachi monument, the Cat deity stone monument venerates cats that control mice (harmful to silkworms). Cat deity monuments are rare nationwide but are notably found in Marumori Town. ** Additionally, within the precincts are various other stone monuments: Ashioyama stele, Aosa Daigongen (青麻大権現), Silk Moth Memorial Monument, Yama no Kami Monument, Yudonosan Stone Monument (for Yudono-san), Kinkazan Stone Monument (for Kinkasan), Amaterasu Kōtaijingū/Konpira Daigongen Stone Monument, Kofune Jinja, and Koyamimine Kami Stone Monument.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Stone shrines of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Various stone monuments at Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Mimachi-shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Mimachi stone monument File:Nekogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Cat deity stone monument </gallery>
* Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine (Silkworm deity Shrine) ** Originally enshrined in the Kasamatsu area and relocated to Sumiyoshi Jinja due to road expansion works. There are three small shrine buildings; the largest enshrines a wooden statue of a slender female deity in a red robe called “Kaiko-gami-sama (Oshira-kami-sama).” Kaiko-gami-sama reflects the history of sericulture in Edano area and is worshipped as a guardian of silkworms, protector against eye diseases, misfortune, and as a guardian deity for children.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Kaikogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine File:Statue of Goddess of Silkworm of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Female deity statue of Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine </gallery>
* Sacred Trees ** Two ancient cedar trees about 700 years old stand along the approach. * Ema-den (Hall of Votive Tablets) ** The Ema-den was repurposed from the old haiden, originally rebuilt in Shōtoku 2, and used until Shōwa 3 (1928).
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Old Sugi trees of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|Ancient cedar trees (~700 years old) File:Emaden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Ema-den of Sumiyoshi Jinja (former haiden) </gallery>

After his death, there was no resident priest, and the shrine was looked after by priests who also served Enkihidakahiko Jinja in Shimada and Kosai Kashima Jinja in Marumori Town’s Kozai area. In Shōwa 28 (1953), the granddaughter-in-law of Onashi Yasunori became the chief priest (gūji), and from that time to the present, the Onashi family has again served as the shrine’s priestly family.
== Precincts == * First and Second Torii Gates ** The area around the first torii gate is farmland, where cloth-impressed pottery shards believed to be from Igu District’s ancient government center (gun-ga) have been excavated. ** Along the approach, there is a small stone shrine (sekishin) dedicated to “Dōso-Ōkami and Ushitora-Inai Myōjin.”
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:First torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|First torii gate of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Douso-ookami and Ushitora-Inai-myoujin shrine.JPG|Stone shrine for Dōso-Ōkami and Ushitora-Inai Myōjin </gallery>
* Shrine buildings (Haiden, Heiden, and Honden in Ichimonsha Nagare-zukuri style) ** The foundation stones (soshiban) of the shrine buildings are partially reused from the Igu District gun-ga site.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|Sumiyoshi Jinja precincts File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Sumiyoshi Jinja precincts File:Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 1.JPG|Haiden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Carving of Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine.JPG|Carving on the Haiden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Honden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|Honden of Sumiyoshi Jinja </gallery>
* Auxiliary Shrines * Stone monument for “Mimachi” (Perpetual Serpent Vigil) ** Mimachi is the festival day for Benzaiten on an auspicious “Kishi (tsuchinoto-mi)” day, venerating the serpent deity. The serpent, as the messenger of Benzaiten, protects silkworm cultivation by controlling mice that harm silkworms, thus revered as a guardian of sericulture. * Cat deity Stone Monument ** Like the Mimachi monument, the Cat deity stone monument venerates cats that control mice (harmful to silkworms). Cat deity monuments are rare nationwide but are notably found in Marumori Town. ** Additionally, within the precincts are various other stone monuments: Ashioyama stele, Aosa Daigongen (青麻大権現), Silk Moth Memorial Monument, Yama no Kami Monument, Yudonosan Stone Monument (for Yudono-san), Kinkazan Stone Monument (for Kinkasan), Amaterasu Kōtaijingū/Konpira Daigongen Stone Monument, Kofune Jinja, and Koyamimine Kami Stone Monument.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Stone shrines of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Various stone monuments at Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Mimachi-shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Mimachi stone monument File:Nekogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Cat deity stone monument </gallery>
* Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine (Silkworm deity Shrine) ** Originally enshrined in the Kasamatsu area and relocated to Sumiyoshi Jinja due to road expansion works. There are three small shrine buildings; the largest enshrines a wooden statue of a slender female deity in a red robe called “Kaiko-gami-sama (Oshira-kami-sama).” Kaiko-gami-sama reflects the history of sericulture in Edano area and is worshipped as a guardian of silkworms, protector against eye diseases, misfortune, and as a guardian deity for children.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Kaikogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine File:Statue of Goddess of Silkworm of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Female deity statue of Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine </gallery>
* Sacred Trees ** Two ancient cedar trees about 700 years old stand along the approach. * Ema-den (Hall of Votive Tablets) ** The Ema-den was repurposed from the old haiden, originally rebuilt in Shōtoku 2, and used until Shōwa 3 (1928).
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Old Sugi trees of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|Ancient cedar trees (~700 years old) File:Emaden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Ema-den of Sumiyoshi Jinja (former haiden) </gallery>

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city closeup.jpg |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sumiyoshi Jinja (Sumiyoshi Shrine) | image = [[File:Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city closeup.jpg|250px]] | caption = Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja Shrine in Kakuda City | map_type = Japan Miyagi | coordinates = {{Coord|37|56|28.6|N|140|47|53.1|E|region:JP-04_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sumiyoshi Jinja}} | location = Eda-no Aza Kōriyama 5, {{ill|Kakuda, Miyagi|ja|角田市|lt=Kakuda City|12=draft}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|12=draft}} | deity = {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|ja|住吉三神|lt=The Three Male Deities of Sumiyoshi|12=draft}} | founded = {{ill|Kareki era|ja|嘉暦元年|lt=Kareki 1 (1326)|12=draft}} | type = Former Village Shrine (Sonsha) | honden = Ichimonsha nagare-zukuri | other_name = Kareki Myōjin, Kareki Myōjin (枯木明神) | festival = {{ill|April 29|ja|4月29日|12=draft}} }}
'''Sumiyoshi Jinja''' (住吉神社, Sumiyoshi Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Kakuda, Miyagi|ja|角田市|lt=Kakuda City|12=draft}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|12=draft}}, Japan. Under the old shrine ranking system, it was a village shrine (sonsha).
== Enshrined Deities == * The shrine enshrines the three male deities of {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|ja|住吉三神|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|12=draft}}: ** Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto (Other name: Iwatsuchi no Kami, Kamichi no O no Kami) ** Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto (Other name: Akatsuchi no Kami, Nakatsuchi no O no Kami) ** Sokotsutsuo no Mikoto (Other name: Sokotsuchi no Kami)
== History == The site where Sumiyoshi Jinja stands is part of the “Kōriyama Site,” believed to have been the location of the administrative center (gun-ga) of Igu District and a temple complex. In a Heisei 17 (2005) excavation of the Kōriyama Site, a talc magatama with a “child-carrying” shape was found in a field adjacent to Sumiyoshi Jinja, indicating that from the early Heian period (8th–9th century) onward, rituals and prayers were likely conducted near the shrine’s current location.
Originally, the shrine was revered under the name “Sumiyoshiyama Iō-ji” during the period of Shinbutsu Konkō (Shinto-Buddhist syncretism). At that time, Sumiyoshiyama Iō-ji enshrined both the Sumiyoshi Sanjin and Yakushi Nyorai as the honji-butsu (Buddhist counterpart of the Shinto deity), serving as a place of healing, warding off illness, and bringing stability to the populace.
On the 29th day of the 3rd month of Kareki 1 (1326), during the reign of Emperor Go-Daigo in the Kamakura period, the divine spirits of the Sumiyoshi Deities were invited (kanjō) from the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|12=draft}} in Settsu Province (present-day Osaka City). This allowed the official use of the name “Sumiyoshi Jinja,” but local people referred to it as “Kareki Myōjin” or “Kareki Myōjin” after the Kareki era. Records indicate that at that time, the shrine enshrined Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto and Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto. During the Kareki era, the Kōriyama area was part of “Omi-gō,” one of the five hamlets of Igu District, and people traveling towards Mutsu Province likely passed near the shrine.
According to the “An’ei Fudoki” (published in An’ei 8 [1780]), around Genroku 14 (1702), a Shugendō practitioner of Onyozan Tōkō-in of Fujio District (now a defunct temple) named Fudō-in Genma invited the Sumiyoshiyama Iō-ji to become a branch temple of Tōkō-in under the name “Honzan-ha Zenkō-in.” Zenkō-in and Sumiyoshi-sha (the shrine) served as a bettōsho (sub-temple or attached temple). Later, Zenkō-in and Sumiyoshi-sha were destroyed by fire, losing all temple-shrine assets and old shrine buildings, leaving no historical records. Under the third head priest Zenkō-in Shinkyō (who died in Meiwa 5 [1768]), the shrine buildings were rebuilt in Shōtoku 2 (1712), and the name was changed to “Sumiyoshi Jinja,” becoming the tutelary shrine (chinju) of the village.
During a 1979 (Shōwa 54) excavation at the Kōriyama Site, remains of buildings facing south with supporting pillars and lotus-patterned round eave tiles (fukuben renge-mon kawara) were found. These discoveries indicated the presence of a fully tiled roof structure in the past, suggesting the prosperity and reverence toward Sumiyoshi Jinja at that time. The family that served as shrine priests, the Onashi family, has a series of ancestral graves bearing titles such as Gon Sōzu and Hōin, indicating they served as Hōin of Zenkō-in (or bettō of Sumiyoshi Jinja) for generations.
In Meiji 13 (1880) August, the Kakuda Prefectural General Affairs Bureau issued an order for the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. Onashi Zenkō, the son of the then Hōin Kenryō, became the shrine’s “shashō” (equivalent to a modern-day chief priest) of Sumiyoshi Jinja in Meiji 15 (1882). Meanwhile, Yakushi-ji, the bettōsho in Hatanaka of the Eda-no area, was separated and headed by Zenkō’s younger brother Kenkaku as Hōin. From this time on, Sumiyoshi Jinja completely separated from the temple. In Meiji 5 (1872) October, Sumiyoshi Jinja was listed as a village shrine, and in Meiji 40 (1907) January, it was designated as a Heihaku Kyōshin Jinja (a shrine receiving offerings from the state).
Onashi Zenkō died in Taishō 3 (1914), and his adopted son Onashi Yasunori became the shashō. Onashi Yasunori served as the principal of Edano Elementary School from Meiji 29 to Taishō 4 (1896-1915). After retiring, he contributed as the shrine priest of Sumiyoshi Jinja, and in Shōwa 3 (1928) he was instrumental in building the current shrine buildings. After his death, there was no resident priest, and the shrine was looked after by priests who also served Enkihidakahiko Jinja in Shimada and Kosai Kashima Jinja in Marumori Town’s Kozai area. In Shōwa 28 (1953), the granddaughter-in-law of Onashi Yasunori became the chief priest (gūji), and from that time to the present, the Onashi family has again served as the shrine’s priestly family.
== Precincts == * First and Second Torii Gates ** The area around the first torii gate is farmland, where cloth-impressed pottery shards believed to be from Igu District’s ancient government center (gun-ga) have been excavated. ** Along the approach, there is a small stone shrine (sekishin) dedicated to “Dōso-Ōkami and Ushitora-Inai Myōjin.”
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:First torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|First torii gate of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Douso-ookami and Ushitora-Inai-myoujin shrine.JPG|Stone shrine for Dōso-Ōkami and Ushitora-Inai Myōjin </gallery>
* Shrine buildings (Haiden, Heiden, and Honden in Ichimonsha Nagare-zukuri style) ** The foundation stones (soshiban) of the shrine buildings are partially reused from the Igu District gun-ga site.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|Sumiyoshi Jinja precincts File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Sumiyoshi Jinja precincts File:Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 1.JPG|Haiden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Carving of Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine.JPG|Carving on the Haiden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Honden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|Honden of Sumiyoshi Jinja </gallery>
* Auxiliary Shrines * Stone monument for “Mimachi” (Perpetual Serpent Vigil) ** Mimachi is the festival day for Benzaiten on an auspicious “Kishi (tsuchinoto-mi)” day, venerating the serpent deity. The serpent, as the messenger of Benzaiten, protects silkworm cultivation by controlling mice that harm silkworms, thus revered as a guardian of sericulture. * Cat deity Stone Monument ** Like the Mimachi monument, the Cat deity stone monument venerates cats that control mice (harmful to silkworms). Cat deity monuments are rare nationwide but are notably found in Marumori Town. ** Additionally, within the precincts are various other stone monuments: Ashioyama stele, Aosa Daigongen (青麻大権現), Silk Moth Memorial Monument, Yama no Kami Monument, Yudonosan Stone Monument (for Yudono-san), Kinkazan Stone Monument (for Kinkasan), Amaterasu Kōtaijingū/Konpira Daigongen Stone Monument, Kofune Jinja, and Koyamimine Kami Stone Monument.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Stone shrines of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Various stone monuments at Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Mimachi-shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Mimachi stone monument File:Nekogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Cat deity stone monument </gallery>
* Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine (Silkworm deity Shrine) ** Originally enshrined in the Kasamatsu area and relocated to Sumiyoshi Jinja due to road expansion works. There are three small shrine buildings; the largest enshrines a wooden statue of a slender female deity in a red robe called “Kaiko-gami-sama (Oshira-kami-sama).” Kaiko-gami-sama reflects the history of sericulture in Edano area and is worshipped as a guardian of silkworms, protector against eye diseases, misfortune, and as a guardian deity for children.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Kaikogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine File:Statue of Goddess of Silkworm of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Female deity statue of Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine </gallery>
* Sacred Trees ** Two ancient cedar trees about 700 years old stand along the approach. * Ema-den (Hall of Votive Tablets) ** The Ema-den was repurposed from the old haiden, originally rebuilt in Shōtoku 2, and used until Shōwa 3 (1928).
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Old Sugi trees of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|Ancient cedar trees (~700 years old) File:Emaden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Ema-den of Sumiyoshi Jinja (former haiden) </gallery>

In Meiji 13 (1880) August, the Kakuda Prefectural General Affairs Bureau issued an order for the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. Onashi Zenkō, the son of the then Hōin Kenryō, became the shrine’s “shashō” (equivalent to a modern-day chief priest) of Sumiyoshi Jinja in Meiji 15 (1882). Meanwhile, Yakushi-ji, the bettōsho in Hatanaka of the Eda-no area, was separated and headed by Zenkō’s younger brother Kenkaku as Hōin. From this time on, Sumiyoshi Jinja completely separated from the temple. In Meiji 5 (1872) October, Sumiyoshi Jinja was listed as a village shrine, and in Meiji 40 (1907) January, it was designated as a Heihaku Kyōshin Jinja (a shrine receiving offerings from the state).
Onashi Zenkō died in Taishō 3 (1914), and his adopted son Onashi Yasunori became the shashō. Onashi Yasunori served as the principal of Edano Elementary School from Meiji 29 to Taishō 4 (1896-1915). After retiring, he contributed as the shrine priest of Sumiyoshi Jinja, and in Shōwa 3 (1928) he was instrumental in building the current shrine buildings. After his death, there was no resident priest, and the shrine was looked after by priests who also served Enkihidakahiko Jinja in Shimada and Kosai Kashima Jinja in Marumori Town’s Kozai area. In Shōwa 28 (1953), the granddaughter-in-law of Onashi Yasunori became the chief priest (gūji), and from that time to the present, the Onashi family has again served as the shrine’s priestly family.
== Precincts == * First and Second Torii Gates ** The area around the first torii gate is farmland, where cloth-impressed pottery shards believed to be from Igu District’s ancient government center (gun-ga) have been excavated. ** Along the approach, there is a small stone shrine (sekishin) dedicated to “Dōso-Ōkami and Ushitora-Inai Myōjin.”
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:First torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|First torii gate of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Douso-ookami and Ushitora-Inai-myoujin shrine.JPG|Stone shrine for Dōso-Ōkami and Ushitora-Inai Myōjin </gallery>
* Shrine buildings (Haiden, Heiden, and Honden in Ichimonsha Nagare-zukuri style) ** The foundation stones (soshiban) of the shrine buildings are partially reused from the Igu District gun-ga site.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|Sumiyoshi Jinja precincts File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Sumiyoshi Jinja precincts File:Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 1.JPG|Haiden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Carving of Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine.JPG|Carving on the Haiden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Honden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|Honden of Sumiyoshi Jinja </gallery>
* Auxiliary Shrines * Stone monument for “Mimachi” (Perpetual Serpent Vigil) ** Mimachi is the festival day for Benzaiten on an auspicious “Kishi (tsuchinoto-mi)” day, venerating the serpent deity. The serpent, as the messenger of Benzaiten, protects silkworm cultivation by controlling mice that harm silkworms, thus revered as a guardian of sericulture. * Cat deity Stone Monument ** Like the Mimachi monument, the Cat deity stone monument venerates cats that control mice (harmful to silkworms). Cat deity monuments are rare nationwide but are notably found in Marumori Town. ** Additionally, within the precincts are various other stone monuments: Ashioyama stele, Aosa Daigongen (青麻大権現), Silk Moth Memorial Monument, Yama no Kami Monument, Yudonosan Stone Monument (for Yudono-san), Kinkazan Stone Monument (for Kinkasan), Amaterasu Kōtaijingū/Konpira Daigongen Stone Monument, Kofune Jinja, and Koyamimine Kami Stone Monument.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Stone shrines of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Various stone monuments at Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Mimachi-shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Mimachi stone monument File:Nekogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Cat deity stone monument </gallery>
* Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine (Silkworm deity Shrine) ** Originally enshrined in the Kasamatsu area and relocated to Sumiyoshi Jinja due to road expansion works. There are three small shrine buildings; the largest enshrines a wooden statue of a slender female deity in a red robe called “Kaiko-gami-sama (Oshira-kami-sama).” Kaiko-gami-sama reflects the history of sericulture in Edano area and is worshipped as a guardian of silkworms, protector against eye diseases, misfortune, and as a guardian deity for children.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Kaikogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine File:Statue of Goddess of Silkworm of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Female deity statue of Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine </gallery>
* Sacred Trees ** Two ancient cedar trees about 700 years old stand along the approach. * Ema-den (Hall of Votive Tablets) ** The Ema-den was repurposed from the old haiden, originally rebuilt in Shōtoku 2, and used until Shōwa 3 (1928).
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Old Sugi trees of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|Ancient cedar trees (~700 years old) File:Emaden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Ema-den of Sumiyoshi Jinja (former haiden) </gallery>
== References == * “Miyagi-ken Kakuda-shi Edano: Sumiyoshi Jinja Goyui-sho” (Sumiyoshi Jinja Shrine Office pamphlet)

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city closeup.jpg |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sumiyoshi Jinja (Sumiyoshi Shrine) | image = [[File:Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city closeup.jpg|250px]] | caption = Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja Shrine in Kakuda City | map_type = Japan Miyagi | coordinates = {{Coord|37|56|28.6|N|140|47|53.1|E|region:JP-04_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sumiyoshi Jinja}} | location = Eda-no Aza Kōriyama 5, {{ill|Kakuda, Miyagi|ja|角田市|lt=Kakuda City|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | deity = {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|ja|住吉三神|lt=The Three Male Deities of Sumiyoshi|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | founded = {{ill|Kareki era|ja|嘉暦元年|lt=Kareki 1 (1326)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | type = Former Village Shrine (Sonsha) | honden = Ichimonsha nagare-zukuri | other_name = Kareki Myōjin, Kareki Myōjin (枯木明神) | festival = {{ill|April 29|ja|4月29日|qq=draft|qq=draft}} }}
'''Sumiyoshi Jinja''' (住吉神社, Sumiyoshi Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Kakuda, Miyagi|ja|角田市|lt=Kakuda City|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan. Under the old shrine Ranking system, it was a village shrine (sonsha).
== Enshrined Deities == * The shrine enshrines the three male deities of {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|ja|住吉三神|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: ** Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto (Other name: Iwatsuchi no Kami, Kamichi no O no Kami) ** Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto (Other name: Akatsuchi no Kami, Nakatsuchi no O no Kami) ** Sokotsutsuo no Mikoto (Other name: Sokotsuchi no Kami)
== History == The site where Sumiyoshi Jinja stands is part of the “Kōriyama Site,” believed to have been the location of the administrative center (gun-ga) of Igu District and a temple complex. In a Heisei 17 (2005) excavation of the Kōriyama Site, a talc magatama with a “child-carrying” shape was found in a field adjacent to Sumiyoshi Jinja, indicating that from the early Heian period (8th–9th century) onward, rituals and prayers were likely conducted near the shrine’s current location.
Originally, the shrine was revered under the name “Sumiyoshiyama Iō-ji” during the period of Shinbutsu Konkō (Shinto-Buddhist syncretism). At that time, Sumiyoshiyama Iō-ji enshrined both the Sumiyoshi Sanjin and Yakushi Nyorai as the honji-butsu (Buddhist counterpart of the Shinto deity), serving as a place of healing, warding off illness, and bringing stability to the populace.
On the 29th day of the 3rd month of Kareki 1 (1326), during the reign of Emperor Go-Daigo in the Kamakura period, the divine spirits of the Sumiyoshi Deities were invited (kanjō) from the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Settsu Province (present-day Osaka City). This allowed the official use of the name “Sumiyoshi Jinja,” but local people referred to it as “Kareki Myōjin” or “Kareki Myōjin” after the Kareki era. Records indicate that at that time, the shrine enshrined Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto and Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto. During the Kareki era, the Kōriyama area was part of “Omi-gō,” one of the five hamlets of Igu District, and people traveling towards Mutsu Province likely passed near the shrine.
According to the “An’ei Fudoki” (published in An’ei 8 [1780]), around Genroku 14 (1702), a Shugendō practitioner of Onyozan Tōkō-in of Fujio District (now a defunct temple) named Fudō-in Genma invited the Sumiyoshiyama Iō-ji to become a branch temple of Tōkō-in under the name “Honzan-ha Zenkō-in.” Zenkō-in and Sumiyoshi-sha (the shrine) served as a bettōsho (sub-temple or attached temple). Later, Zenkō-in and Sumiyoshi-sha were destroyed by fire, losing all temple-shrine assets and old shrine buildings, leaving no historical records. Under the third head priest Zenkō-in Shinkyō (who died in Meiwa 5 [1768]), the shrine buildings were rebuilt in Shōtoku 2 (1712), and the name was changed to “Sumiyoshi Jinja,” becoming the tutelary shrine (chinju) of the village.
During a 1979 (Shōwa 54) excavation at the Kōriyama Site, remains of buildings facing south with supporting pillars and lotus-patterned round eave tiles (fukuben renge-mon kawara) were found. These discoveries indicated the presence of a fully tiled roof structure in the past, suggesting the prosperity and reverence toward Sumiyoshi Jinja at that time. The family that served as shrine priests, the Onashi family, has a series of ancestral graves bearing titles such as Gon Sōzu and Hōin, indicating they served as Hōin of Zenkō-in (or bettō of Sumiyoshi Jinja) for generations.
In Meiji 13 (1880) August, the Kakuda Prefectural General Affairs Bureau issued an order for the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. Onashi Zenkō, the son of the then Hōin Kenryō, became the shrine’s “shashō” (equivalent to a modern-day chief priest) of Sumiyoshi Jinja in Meiji 15 (1882). Meanwhile, Yakushi-ji, the bettōsho in Hatanaka of the Eda-no area, was separated and headed by Zenkō’s younger brother Kenkaku as Hōin. From this time on, Sumiyoshi Jinja completely separated from the temple. In Meiji 5 (1872) October, Sumiyoshi Jinja was listed as a village shrine, and in Meiji 40 (1907) January, it was designated as a Heihaku Kyōshin Jinja (a shrine receiving offerings from the state).
Onashi Zenkō died in Taishō 3 (1914), and his adopted son Onashi Yasunori became the shashō. Onashi Yasunori served as the principal of Edano Elementary School from Meiji 29 to Taishō 4 (1896-1915). After retiring, he contributed as the shrine priest of Sumiyoshi Jinja, and in Shōwa 3 (1928) he was instrumental in building the current shrine buildings. After his death, there was no resident priest, and the shrine was looked after by priests who also served Enkihidakahiko Jinja in Shimada and Kosai Kashima Jinja in Marumori Town’s Kozai area. In Shōwa 28 (1953), the granddaughter-in-law of Onashi Yasunori became the chief priest (gūji), and from that time to the present, the Onashi family has again served as the shrine’s priestly family.
== Precincts == * First and Second Torii Gates ** The area around the first torii gate is farmland, where cloth-impressed pottery shards believed to be from Igu District’s ancient government center (gun-ga) have been excavated. ** Along the approach, there is a small stone shrine (sekishin) dedicated to “Dōso-Ōkami and Ushitora-Inai Myōjin.”
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:First torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|First torii gate of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Douso-ookami and Ushitora-Inai-myoujin shrine.JPG|Stone shrine for Dōso-Ōkami and Ushitora-Inai Myōjin </gallery>
* Shrine buildings (Haiden, Heiden, and Honden in Ichimonsha Nagare-zukuri style) ** The foundation stones (soshiban) of the shrine buildings are partially reused from the Igu District gun-ga site.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|Sumiyoshi Jinja precincts File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Sumiyoshi Jinja precincts File:Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 1.JPG|Haiden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Carving of Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine.JPG|Carving on the Haiden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Honden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|Honden of Sumiyoshi Jinja </gallery>
* Auxiliary Shrines * Stone monument for “Mimachi” (Perpetual Serpent Vigil) ** Mimachi is the festival day for Benzaiten on an auspicious “Kishi (tsuchinoto-mi)” day, venerating the serpent deity. The serpent, as the messenger of Benzaiten, protects silkworm cultivation by controlling mice that harm silkworms, thus revered as a guardian of sericulture. * Cat deity Stone Monument ** Like the Mimachi monument, the Cat deity stone monument venerates cats that control mice (harmful to silkworms). Cat deity monuments are rare nationwide but are notably found in Marumori Town. ** Additionally, within the precincts are various other stone monuments: Ashioyama stele, Aosa Daigongen (青麻大権現), Silk Moth Memorial Monument, Yama no Kami Monument, Yudonosan Stone Monument (for Yudono-san), Kinkazan Stone Monument (for Kinkasan), Amaterasu Kōtaijingū/Konpira Daigongen Stone Monument, Kofune Jinja, and Koyamimine Kami Stone Monument.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Stone shrines of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Various stone monuments at Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Mimachi-shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Mimachi stone monument File:Nekogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Cat deity stone monument </gallery>
* Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine (Silkworm deity Shrine) ** Originally enshrined in the Kasamatsu area and relocated to Sumiyoshi Jinja due to road expansion works. There are three small shrine buildings; the largest enshrines a wooden statue of a slender female deity in a red robe called “Kaiko-gami-sama (Oshira-kami-sama).” Kaiko-gami-sama reflects the history of sericulture in Edano area and is worshipped as a guardian of silkworms, protector against eye diseases, misfortune, and as a guardian deity for children.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Kaikogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine File:Statue of Goddess of Silkworm of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Female deity statue of Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine </gallery>
* Sacred Trees ** Two ancient cedar trees about 700 years old stand along the approach. * Ema-den (Hall of Votive Tablets) ** The Ema-den was repurposed from the old haiden, originally rebuilt in Shōtoku 2, and used until Shōwa 3 (1928).
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Old Sugi trees of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|Ancient cedar trees (~700 years old) File:Emaden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Ema-den of Sumiyoshi Jinja (former haiden) </gallery>
== References == * “Miyagi-ken Kakuda-shi Edano: Sumiyoshi Jinja Goyui-sho” (Sumiyoshi Jinja Shrine Office pamphlet)

revision.comment: 14 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:住吉神社 (角田市) up to rev 100086987
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称=住吉神社 |画像=[[File:Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city closeup.jpg|250px|Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2]] |所在地=[[宮城県]][[角田市]]枝野字郡山5 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|56|28.6|N|140|47|53.1|E|region:JP-04_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=住吉神社}} |祭神=[[住吉三神|男命三神]] |社格=村社 |創建=[[嘉暦]]元年([[1326年]]) |本殿=一間社流造 |別名=嘉暦明神
枯木明神 |例祭=[[4月29日]] |地図={{Location map|Japan Miyagi|lat_deg=37|lat_min=56|lat_sec=28.6|lon_deg=140|lon_min=47|lon_sec=53.1|label=住吉神社|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''住吉神社'''（すみよしじんじゃ）は、[[宮城県]][[角田市]]にある[[神社]]である。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は村社。 ==祭神== *表筒之男命（別名：磐土神、上地廼雄神）、中筒之男命（別名：赤土神、中地廼雄神）、底筒之男命（別名：底土神）の[[住吉三神|男命三神]]を祀る。 ==由緒== 住吉神社の鎮座する地は、伊具郡衙や寺院跡とされる「郡山遺跡」の一部になっている。[[平成]]17年度に行われた郡山遺跡の発掘調査において、住吉神社に隣接する畑からは「滑石子持ち[[勾玉]]」が出土しており、住吉神社付近では平安時代前半（8 - 9世紀頃）には神事や祈願が行われていたと推測されている。 住吉神社は、創建当初は「住吉山医王寺」という山号で崇敬されていた。神仏混合の時代であったため、住吉山医王寺では住吉三神と本地仏である[[薬師如来]]を祀り、病気の治療や民衆の安定を図る場所となっていた。 鎌倉時代の[[後醍醐天皇]]の御代である[[嘉暦]]元年（[[1326年]]）[[3月29日]]、[[摂津国]]（現在の大阪市）に鎮座する[[住吉大社|住吉神社]]より住吉三神の御分霊を勧請したことから、'''「住吉神社」'''の名称が正式に使えるようになったが、土地の人々からは勧請された年号にちなみ「嘉暦明神」や「枯木明神」と呼ばれ、祭神も住吉三神の上地廼雄命（表筒男命）と中地廼雄命（中筒男命）を祀っていたと記録されている。嘉暦年間の郡山地区は「麻績郷」という伊具郡五郷のうちのひとつになっており、神社付近も陸奥国へ向かう人々などの往来があったという。 [[安永]]8年（[[1780年]]）に刊行された『安永風土記』によれば、[[元禄]]14年頃（[[1702年]]）に、藤尾地区大門にあった鬼越山東光院（現在廃寺）の修験者である不動院元真が住吉山医王寺に東光院の末寺として「本山派善光院」を開院し、住吉社と薬師寺を別当所とした。善光院と住吉社はその後火災にあい、寺社の財産や当時の社殿は全て焼失しており、記録資料は残されていないという。院主三代目の善光院真清（[[明和]]5年（[[1768年]]）の没）代の[[正徳 (日本)|正徳]]2年（[[1712年]]）には焼失した社殿を再興して、名称を「住吉神社」として村の鎮守社となった。 [[昭和]]54年（[[1979年]]）に行われた郡山遺跡の発掘調査では、[[掘立柱建物|総柱式]]の南面する建物跡や「複弁蓮華文軒丸瓦」が発掘された。これらは本格的な瓦葺きの建物があったことを示す証拠であり、当時の住吉神社の隆盛と崇敬ぶりをうかがえる資料とされた。住吉神社の神職家である小梨家の歴代墓所には、権僧都や法印の名が残されており、小梨家は代々、善光院の法印（もしくは住吉神社の別当）を勤めていたとされる。 [[明治]]13年（[[1880年]]）8月、角田県庶務局により神仏分離の通達が出された。当時の法印である法印賢了の子であった小梨善光は、明治15年（1882年）に住吉神社の社掌（現在の宮司にあたる）となり、別当所である枝野地区畑中の薬師寺は善光の弟の賢学が法印となり分立した。その後、小梨善光は現在の住吉神社へ至る基礎を築き上げ、この時をもって住吉神社は寺院と完全に分離した。明治5年（[[1872年]]）10月、住吉神社は村社に列せられ、明治40年（[[1907年]]）1月に幣帛供進神社に指定された。 小梨善光は[[大正]]3年（[[1914年]]）に没するが、小梨家に養子となった小梨保則が社掌となった。小梨保則は明治29年から大正4年まで枝野小学校の 校長を務め、退職後は住吉神社の神職として貢献し、昭和3年（1928年）には現在の住吉神社社殿の建立に尽力した。小梨保則の没後は神職不在となり、島田地区に鎮座する延喜式内社の[[熱日高彦神社]]や丸森町小斎地区に鎮座の小斎鹿島神社の兼務社となったが、昭和28年に小梨保則の孫嫁が宮司となってから現在に至るまで小梨家が再び住吉神社の社家として奉仕している。 ==境内== *第一鳥居・第二鳥居 **第一鳥居の周囲は畑になっており、参道や畑からは伊具郡衙に使われていたとされる布目瓦などが現在でも発掘されている。 **参道の途中には「道祖大神・丑寅居内明神」という石祠が祀られている。 File:First torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|住吉神社第一鳥居 File:Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|住吉神社第二鳥居 File:Douso-ookami and Ushitora-Inai-myoujin shrine.JPG|道祖大神・丑寅居内明神の石祠 *社殿（拝殿・幣殿・一間社流造の本殿） **社殿の礎石などには、伊具郡衙とされる礎石の一部が流用されている。 File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|住吉神社境内 File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|住吉神社境内 File:Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 1.JPG|住吉神社拝殿 File:Carving of Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine.JPG|住吉神社拝殿の彫刻 File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|住吉神社本殿 File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|住吉神社本殿 *境内社 *[[巳待塔|巳待の石碑]]：巳待は己巳（つちのとのみ）の日に行う[[弁財天]]の縁日で、蛇の神を祀る石碑である。蛇は弁財天の[[神使]]とされ、また、蚕を食べるネズミを退治してくれるため養蚕の守護神とされている。 *猫神の石碑：巳待の石碑と同じく、蚕の害となるねずみを退治する猫の霊を祀るもの。猫神の石碑は全国でも丸森町に特に多い珍しいものである。 **その他にも境内には、足尾山石碑、[[青麻神社|青麻大権現]]、蚕供養碑、山神碑、[[湯殿山神社|湯殿山石碑]]、[[黄金山神社 (石巻市)|金華山石碑]]、天照皇太神宮・金毘羅大権現石碑、[[古峯神社]]、蚕養峰神の石碑などが祀られている。 File:Stone shrines of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|住吉神社の石碑群 File:Mimachi-shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|巳待の石碑 File:Nekogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|猫神の石碑 *蚕神様社：もとは笠松地区に鎮座していたものを道路拡張工事に伴い住吉神社へ遷座した。社殿は三社あり、そのうち一番大きなものには「蚕神様（おしらかみさま）」と呼ばれる朱色の長衣を身につけた細身の女神の木像が祀られている。蚕神様は枝野地区の養蚕を物語るもので、養蚕の神として、その他にも眼病予防や災難除け、子供の守り神として信仰されているという。 File:Kaikogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|蚕神様社 File:Statue of Goddess of Silkworm of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|蚕神様社の女神像 *御神木：樹齢約700年の老杉の大木が2本、境内参道脇に立っている。 *絵馬堂：[[正徳 (日本)|正徳]]2年に再築され[[昭和]]3年まで使用されていた旧拝殿を流用したものである。 File:Old Sugi trees of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|住吉神社の大杉（樹齢約700年） File:Emaden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|住吉神社絵馬殿（旧拝殿） ==参考文献== *『宮城県角田市枝野　住吉神社御由緒』（住吉神社社務所） == 外部リンク == * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010234 住吉神社]（宮城県[[神社庁]]） {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すみよししんしや かくたし}} revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[住吉神社 (角田市)]] to [[Sumiyoshi Shrine (Miyagi)]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称=住吉神社 |画像=[[File:Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city closeup.jpg|250px|Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2]] |所在地=[[宮城県]][[角田市]]枝野字郡山5 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|56|28.6|N|140|47|53.1|E|region:JP-04_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=住吉神社}} |祭神=[[住吉三神|男命三神]] |社格=村社 |創建=[[嘉暦]]元年([[1326年]]) |本殿=一間社流造 |別名=嘉暦明神
枯木明神 |例祭=[[4月29日]] |地図={{Location map|Japan Miyagi|lat_deg=37|lat_min=56|lat_sec=28.6|lon_deg=140|lon_min=47|lon_sec=53.1|label=住吉神社|position=center|width=200|float=center}} }} '''住吉神社'''（すみよしじんじゃ）は、[[宮城県]][[角田市]]にある[[神社]]である。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は村社。 ==祭神== *表筒之男命（別名：磐土神、上地廼雄神）、中筒之男命（別名：赤土神、中地廼雄神）、底筒之男命（別名：底土神）の[[住吉三神|男命三神]]を祀る。 ==由緒== 住吉神社の鎮座する地は、伊具郡衙や寺院跡とされる「郡山遺跡」の一部になっている。[[平成]]17年度に行われた郡山遺跡の発掘調査において、住吉神社に隣接する畑からは「滑石子持ち[[勾玉]]」が出土しており、住吉神社付近では平安時代前半（8 - 9世紀頃）には神事や祈願が行われていたと推測されている。 住吉神社は、創建当初は「住吉山医王寺」という山号で崇敬されていた。神仏混合の時代であったため、住吉山医王寺では住吉三神と本地仏である[[薬師如来]]を祀り、病気の治療や民衆の安定を図る場所となっていた。 鎌倉時代の[[後醍醐天皇]]の御代である[[嘉暦]]元年（[[1326年]]）[[3月29日]]、[[摂津国]]（現在の大阪市）に鎮座する[[住吉大社|住吉神社]]より住吉三神の御分霊を勧請したことから、'''「住吉神社」'''の名称が正式に使えるようになったが、土地の人々からは勧請された年号にちなみ「嘉暦明神」や「枯木明神」と呼ばれ、祭神も住吉三神の上地廼雄命（表筒男命）と中地廼雄命（中筒男命）を祀っていたと記録されている。嘉暦年間の郡山地区は「麻績郷」という伊具郡五郷のうちのひとつになっており、神社付近も陸奥国へ向かう人々などの往来があったという。 [[安永]]8年（[[1780年]]）に刊行された『安永風土記』によれば、[[元禄]]14年頃（[[1702年]]）に、藤尾地区大門にあった鬼越山東光院（現在廃寺）の修験者である不動院元真が住吉山医王寺に東光院の末寺として「本山派善光院」を開院し、住吉社と薬師寺を別当所とした。善光院と住吉社はその後火災にあい、寺社の財産や当時の社殿は全て焼失しており、記録資料は残されていないという。院主三代目の善光院真清（[[明和]]5年（[[1768年]]）の没）代の[[正徳 (日本)|正徳]]2年（[[1712年]]）には焼失した社殿を再興して、名称を「住吉神社」として村の鎮守社となった。 [[昭和]]54年（[[1979年]]）に行われた郡山遺跡の発掘調査では、[[掘立柱建物|総柱式]]の南面する建物跡や「複弁蓮華文軒丸瓦」が発掘された。これらは本格的な瓦葺きの建物があったことを示す証拠であり、当時の住吉神社の隆盛と崇敬ぶりをうかがえる資料とされた。住吉神社の神職家である小梨家の歴代墓所には、権僧都や法印の名が残されており、小梨家は代々、善光院の法印（もしくは住吉神社の別当）を勤めていたとされる。 [[明治]]13年（[[1880年]]）8月、角田県庶務局により神仏分離の通達が出された。当時の法印である法印賢了の子であった小梨善光は、明治15年（1882年）に住吉神社の社掌（現在の宮司にあたる）となり、別当所である枝野地区畑中の薬師寺は善光の弟の賢学が法印となり分立した。その後、小梨善光は現在の住吉神社へ至る基礎を築き上げ、この時をもって住吉神社は寺院と完全に分離した。明治5年（[[1872年]]）10月、住吉神社は村社に列せられ、明治40年（[[1907年]]）1月に幣帛供進神社に指定された。 小梨善光は[[大正]]3年（[[1914年]]）に没するが、小梨家に養子となった小梨保則が社掌となった。小梨保則は明治29年から大正4年まで枝野小学校の 校長を務め、退職後は住吉神社の神職として貢献し、昭和3年（1928年）には現在の住吉神社社殿の建立に尽力した。小梨保則の没後は神職不在となり、島田地区に鎮座する延喜式内社の[[熱日高彦神社]]や丸森町小斎地区に鎮座の小斎鹿島神社の兼務社となったが、昭和28年に小梨保則の孫嫁が宮司となってから現在に至るまで小梨家が再び住吉神社の社家として奉仕している。 ==境内== *第一鳥居・第二鳥居 **第一鳥居の周囲は畑になっており、参道や畑からは伊具郡衙に使われていたとされる布目瓦などが現在でも発掘されている。 **参道の途中には「道祖大神・丑寅居内明神」という石祠が祀られている。 File:First torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|住吉神社第一鳥居 File:Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|住吉神社第二鳥居 File:Douso-ookami and Ushitora-Inai-myoujin shrine.JPG|道祖大神・丑寅居内明神の石祠 *社殿（拝殿・幣殿・一間社流造の本殿） **社殿の礎石などには、伊具郡衙とされる礎石の一部が流用されている。 File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|住吉神社境内 File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|住吉神社境内 File:Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 1.JPG|住吉神社拝殿 File:Carving of *[[巳待塔|巳待の石碑]]：巳待は己巳（つちのとのみ）の日に行う[[弁財天]]の縁日で、蛇の神を祀る石碑である。蛇は弁財天の[[神使]]とされ、また、蚕を食べるネズミを退治してくれるため養蚕の守護神とされている。 *猫神の石碑：巳待の石碑と同じく、蚕の害となるねずみを退治する猫の霊を祀るもの。猫神の石碑は全国でも丸森町に特に多い珍しいものである。 **その他にも境内には、足尾山石碑、[[青麻神社|青麻大権現]]、蚕供養碑、山神碑、[[湯殿山神社|湯殿山石碑]]、[[黄金山神社 (石巻市)|金華山石碑]]、天照皇太神宮・金毘羅大権現石碑、[[古峯神社]]、蚕養峰神の石碑などが祀られている。 File:Stone shrines of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|住吉神社の石碑群 File:Mimachi-shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|巳待の石碑 File:Nekogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|猫神の石碑 *蚕神様社：もとは笠松地区に鎮座していたものを道路拡張工事に伴い住吉神社へ遷座した。社殿は三社あり、そのうち一番大きなものには「蚕神様（おしらかみさま）」と呼ばれる朱色の長衣を身につけた細身の女神の木像が祀られている。蚕神様は枝野地区の養蚕を物語るもので、養蚕の神として、その他にも眼病予防や災難除け、子供の守り神として信仰されているという。 File:Kaikogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|蚕神様社 File:Statue of Goddess of Silkworm of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|蚕神様社の女神像 *御神木：樹齢約700年の老杉の大木が2本、境内参道脇に立っている。 *絵馬堂：[[正徳 (日本)|正徳]]2年に再築され[[昭和]]3年まで使用されていた旧拝殿を流用したものである。 File:Old Sugi trees of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|住吉神社の大杉（樹齢約700年） File:Emaden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|住吉神社絵馬殿（旧拝殿） '''Sumiyoshi Jinja''' (住吉神社, Sumiyoshi Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Kakuda, Miyagi|ja|角田市|lt=Kakuda City|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan. Under the old shrine Ranking system, it was a village shrine (sonsha).
== Enshrined Deities == * The shrine enshrines the three male deities of {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|ja|住吉三神|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: ** Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto (Other name: Iwatsuchi no Kami, Kamichi no O no Kami) ** Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto (Other name: Akatsuchi no Kami, Nakatsuchi no O no Kami) ** Sokotsutsuo no Mikoto (Other name: Sokotsuchi no Kami)
== History == The site where Sumiyoshi Jinja stands is part of the “Kōriyama Site,” believed to have been the location of the administrative center (gun-ga) of Igu District and a temple complex. In a Heisei 17 (2005) excavation of the Kōriyama Site, a talc magatama with a “child-carrying” shape was found in a field adjacent to Sumiyoshi Jinja, indicating that from the early Heian period (8th–9th century) onward, rituals and prayers were likely conducted near the shrine’s current location.
Originally, the shrine was revered under the name “Sumiyoshiyama Iō-ji” during the period of Shinbutsu Konkō (Shinto-Buddhist syncretism). At that time, Sumiyoshiyama Iō-ji enshrined both the Sumiyoshi Sanjin and Yakushi Nyorai as the honji-butsu (Buddhist counterpart of the Shinto deity), serving as a place of healing, warding off illness, and bringing stability to the populace.
On the 29th day of the 3rd month of Kareki 1 (1326), during the reign of Emperor Go-Daigo in the Kamakura period, the divine spirits of the Sumiyoshi Deities were invited (kanjō) from the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Settsu Province (present-day Osaka City). This allowed the official use of the name “Sumiyoshi Jinja,” but local people referred to it as “Kareki Myōjin” or “Kareki Myōjin” after the Kareki era. Records indicate that at that time, the shrine enshrined Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto and Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto. During the Kareki era, the Kōriyama area was part of “Omi-gō,” one of the five hamlets of Igu District, and people traveling towards Mutsu Province likely passed near the shrine.
According to the “An’ei Fudoki” (published in An’ei 8 [1780]), around Genroku 14 (1702), a Shugendō practitioner of Onyozan Tōkō-in of Fujio District (now a defunct temple) named Fudō-in Genma invited the Sumiyoshiyama Iō-ji to become a branch temple of Tōkō-in under the name “Honzan-ha Zenkō-in.” Zenkō-in and Sumiyoshi-sha (the shrine) served as a bettōsho (sub-temple or attached temple). Later, Zenkō-in and Sumiyoshi-sha were destroyed by fire, losing all temple-shrine assets and old shrine buildings, leaving no historical records. Under the third head priest Zenkō-in Shinkyō (who died in Meiwa 5 [1768]), the shrine buildings were rebuilt in Shōtoku 2 (1712), and the name was changed to “Sumiyoshi Jinja,” becoming the tutelary shrine (chinju) of the village.
During a 1979 (Shōwa 54) excavation at the Kōriyama Site, remains of buildings facing south with supporting pillars and lotus-patterned round eave tiles (fukuben renge-mon kawara) were found. These discoveries indicated the presence of a fully tiled roof structure in the past, suggesting the prosperity and reverence toward Sumiyoshi Jinja at that time. The family that served as shrine priests, the Onashi family, has a series of ancestral graves bearing titles such as Gon Sōzu and Hōin, indicating they served as Hōin of Zenkō-in (or bettō of Sumiyoshi Jinja) for generations.
In Meiji 13 (1880) August, the Kakuda Prefectural General Affairs Bureau issued an order for the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. Onashi Zenkō, the son of the then Hōin Kenryō, became the shrine’s “shashō” (equivalent to a modern-day chief priest) of Sumiyoshi Jinja in Meiji 15 (1882). Meanwhile, Yakushi-ji, the bettōsho in Hatanaka of the Eda-no area, was separated and headed by Zenkō’s younger brother Kenkaku as Hōin. From this time on, Sumiyoshi Jinja completely separated from the temple. In Meiji 5 (1872) October, Sumiyoshi Jinja was listed as a village shrine, and in Meiji 40 (1907) January, it was designated as a Heihaku Kyōshin Jinja (a shrine receiving offerings from the state).
Onashi Zenkō died in Taishō 3 (1914), and his adopted son Onashi Yasunori became the shashō. Onashi Yasunori served as the principal of Edano Elementary School from Meiji 29 to Taishō 4 (1896-1915). After retiring, he contributed as the shrine priest of Sumiyoshi Jinja, and in Shōwa 3 (1928) he was instrumental in building the current shrine buildings. After his death, there was no resident priest, and the shrine was looked after by priests who also served Enkihidakahiko Jinja in Shimada and Kosai Kashima Jinja in Marumori Town’s Kozai area. In Shōwa 28 (1953), the granddaughter-in-law of Onashi Yasunori became the chief priest (gūji), and from that time to the present, the Onashi family has again served as the shrine’s priestly family.
== Precincts == * First and Second Torii Gates ** The area around the first torii gate is farmland, where cloth-impressed pottery shards believed to be from Igu District’s ancient government center (gun-ga) have been excavated. ** Along the approach, there is a small stone shrine (sekishin) dedicated to “Dōso-Ōkami and Ushitora-Inai Myōjin.”
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:First torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|First torii gate of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Douso-ookami and Ushitora-Inai-myoujin shrine.JPG|Stone shrine for Dōso-Ōkami and Ushitora-Inai Myōjin </gallery>
* Shrine buildings (Haiden, Heiden, and Honden in Ichimonsha Nagare-zukuri style) ** The foundation stones (soshiban) of the shrine buildings are partially reused from the Igu District gun-ga site.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|Sumiyoshi Jinja precincts File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Sumiyoshi Jinja precincts File:Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 1.JPG|Haiden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Carving of Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine.JPG|Carving on the Haiden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Honden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|Honden of Sumiyoshi Jinja </gallery>
* Auxiliary Shrines * Stone monument for “Mimachi” (Perpetual Serpent Vigil) ** Mimachi is the festival day for Benzaiten on an auspicious “Kishi (tsuchinoto-mi)” day, venerating the serpent deity. The serpent, as the messenger of Benzaiten, protects silkworm cultivation by controlling mice that harm silkworms, thus revered as a guardian of sericulture. * Cat deity Stone Monument ** Like the Mimachi monument, the Cat deity stone monument venerates cats that control mice (harmful to silkworms). Cat deity monuments are rare nationwide but are notably found in Marumori Town. ** Additionally, within the precincts are various other stone monuments: Ashioyama stele, Aosa Daigongen (青麻大権現), Silk Moth Memorial Monument, Yama no Kami Monument, Yudonosan Stone Monument (for Yudono-san), Kinkazan Stone Monument (for Kinkasan), Amaterasu Kōtaijingū/Konpira Daigongen Stone Monument, Kofune Jinja, and Koyamimine Kami Stone Monument.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Stone shrines of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Various stone monuments at Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Mimachi-shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Mimachi stone monument File:Nekogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Cat deity stone monument </gallery>
* Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine (Silkworm deity Shrine) ** Originally enshrined in the Kasamatsu area and relocated to Sumiyoshi Jinja due to road expansion works. There are three small shrine buildings; the largest enshrines a wooden statue of a slender female deity in a red robe called “Kaiko-gami-sama (Oshira-kami-sama).” Kaiko-gami-sama reflects the history of sericulture in Edano area and is worshipped as a guardian of silkworms, protector against eye diseases, misfortune, and as a guardian deity for children.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Kaikogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine File:Statue of Goddess of Silkworm of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Female deity statue of Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine </gallery>
* Sacred Trees ** Two ancient cedar trees about 700 years old stand along the approach. * Ema-den (Hall of Votive Tablets) ** The Ema-den was repurposed from the old haiden, originally rebuilt in Shōtoku 2, and used until Shōwa 3 (1928).
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Old Sugi trees of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|Ancient cedar trees (~700 years old) File:Emaden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Ema-den of Sumiyoshi Jinja (former haiden) </gallery>
== References == * “Miyagi-ken Kakuda-shi Edano: Sumiyoshi Jinja Goyui-sho” (Sumiyoshi Jinja Shrine Office pamphlet)
== External Links == * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010234 Sumiyoshi Jinja] (Miyagi Prefecture Jinja-chō)

{{draft}}
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city closeup.jpg |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sumiyoshi Jinja (Sumiyoshi Shrine) | image = [[File:Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city closeup.jpg|250px]] | caption = Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja Shrine in Kakuda City | map_type = Japan Miyagi | coordinates = {{Coord|37|56|28.6|N|140|47|53.1|E|region:JP-04_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Sumiyoshi Jinja}} | location = Eda-no Aza Kōriyama 5, {{ill|Kakuda, Miyagi|ja|角田市|lt=Kakuda City|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | deity = {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|ja|住吉三神|lt=The Three Male Deities of Sumiyoshi|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | founded = {{ill|Kareki era|ja|嘉暦元年|lt=Kareki 1 (1326)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | type = Former Village Shrine (Sonsha) | honden = Ichimonsha nagare-zukuri | other_name = Kareki Myōjin, Kareki Myōjin (枯木明神) | festival = {{ill|April 29|ja|4月29日|qq=draft|qq=draft}} }}
'''Sumiyoshi Jinja''' (住吉神社, Sumiyoshi Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Kakuda, Miyagi|ja|角田市|lt=Kakuda City|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan. Under the old shrine Ranking system, it was a village shrine (sonsha).
== Enshrined Deities == * The shrine enshrines the three male deities of {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|ja|住吉三神|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: ** Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto (Other name: Iwatsuchi no Kami, Kamichi no O no Kami) ** Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto (Other name: Akatsuchi no Kami, Nakatsuchi no O no Kami) ** Sokotsutsuo no Mikoto (Other name: Sokotsuchi no Kami)
== History == The site where Sumiyoshi Jinja stands is part of the “Kōriyama Site,” believed to have been the location of the administrative center (gun-ga) of Igu District and a temple complex. In a Heisei 17 (2005) excavation of the Kōriyama Site, a talc magatama with a “child-carrying” shape was found in a field adjacent to Sumiyoshi Jinja, indicating that from the early Heian period (8th–9th century) onward, rituals and prayers were likely conducted near the shrine’s current location.
Originally, the shrine was revered under the name “Sumiyoshiyama Iō-ji” during the period of Shinbutsu Konkō (Shinto-Buddhist syncretism). At that time, Sumiyoshiyama Iō-ji enshrined both the Sumiyoshi Sanjin and Yakushi Nyorai as the honji-butsu (Buddhist counterpart of the Shinto deity), serving as a place of healing, warding off illness, and bringing stability to the populace.
On the 29th day of the 3rd month of Kareki 1 (1326), during the reign of Emperor Go-Daigo in the Kamakura period, the divine spirits of the Sumiyoshi Deities were invited (kanjō) from the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Settsu Province (present-day Osaka City). This allowed the official use of the name “Sumiyoshi Jinja,” but local people referred to it as “Kareki Myōjin” or “Kareki Myōjin” after the Kareki era. Records indicate that at that time, the shrine enshrined Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto and Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto. During the Kareki era, the Kōriyama area was part of “Omi-gō,” one of the five hamlets of Igu District, and people traveling towards Mutsu Province likely passed near the shrine.
According to the “An’ei Fudoki” (published in An’ei 8 [1780]), around Genroku 14 (1702), a Shugendō practitioner of Onyozan Tōkō-in of Fujio District (now a defunct temple) named Fudō-in Genma invited the Sumiyoshiyama Iō-ji to become a branch temple of Tōkō-in under the name “Honzan-ha Zenkō-in.” Zenkō-in and Sumiyoshi-sha (the shrine) served as a bettōsho (sub-temple or attached temple). Later, Zenkō-in and Sumiyoshi-sha were destroyed by fire, losing all temple-shrine assets and old shrine buildings, leaving no historical records. Under the third head priest Zenkō-in Shinkyō (who died in Meiwa 5 [1768]), the shrine buildings were rebuilt in Shōtoku 2 (1712), and the name was changed to “Sumiyoshi Jinja,” becoming the tutelary shrine (chinju) of the village.
During a 1979 (Shōwa 54) excavation at the Kōriyama Site, remains of buildings facing south with supporting pillars and lotus-patterned round eave tiles (fukuben renge-mon kawara) were found. These discoveries indicated the presence of a fully tiled roof structure in the past, suggesting the prosperity and reverence toward Sumiyoshi Jinja at that time. The family that served as shrine priests, the Onashi family, has a series of ancestral graves bearing titles such as Gon Sōzu and Hōin, indicating they served as Hōin of Zenkō-in (or bettō of Sumiyoshi Jinja) for generations.
In Meiji 13 (1880) August, the Kakuda Prefectural General Affairs Bureau issued an order for the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. Onashi Zenkō, the son of the then Hōin Kenryō, became the shrine’s “shashō” (equivalent to a modern-day chief priest) of Sumiyoshi Jinja in Meiji 15 (1882). Meanwhile, Yakushi-ji, the bettōsho in Hatanaka of the Eda-no area, was separated and headed by Zenkō’s younger brother Kenkaku as Hōin. From this time on, Sumiyoshi Jinja completely separated from the temple. In Meiji 5 (1872) October, Sumiyoshi Jinja was listed as a village shrine, and in Meiji 40 (1907) January, it was designated as a Heihaku Kyōshin Jinja (a shrine receiving offerings from the state).
Onashi Zenkō died in Taishō 3 (1914), and his adopted son Onashi Yasunori became the shashō. Onashi Yasunori served as the principal of Edano Elementary School from Meiji 29 to Taishō 4 (1896-1915). After retiring, he contributed as the shrine priest of Sumiyoshi Jinja, and in Shōwa 3 (1928) he was instrumental in building the current shrine buildings. After his death, there was no resident priest, and the shrine was looked after by priests who also served Enkihidakahiko Jinja in Shimada and Kosai Kashima Jinja in Marumori Town’s Kozai area. In Shōwa 28 (1953), the granddaughter-in-law of Onashi Yasunori became the chief priest (gūji), and from that time to the present, the Onashi family has again served as the shrine’s priestly family.
== Precincts == * First and Second Torii Gates ** The area around the first torii gate is farmland, where cloth-impressed pottery shards believed to be from Igu District’s ancient government center (gun-ga) have been excavated. ** Along the approach, there is a small stone shrine (sekishin) dedicated to “Dōso-Ōkami and Ushitora-Inai Myōjin.”
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:First torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|First torii gate of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Second torii gate of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Douso-ookami and Ushitora-Inai-myoujin shrine.JPG|Stone shrine for Dōso-Ōkami and Ushitora-Inai Myōjin </gallery>
* Shrine buildings (Haiden, Heiden, and Honden in Ichimonsha Nagare-zukuri style) ** The foundation stones (soshiban) of the shrine buildings are partially reused from the Igu District gun-ga site.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|Sumiyoshi Jinja precincts File:Precincts of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Sumiyoshi Jinja precincts File:Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 1.JPG|Haiden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Carving of Haiden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine.JPG|Carving on the Haiden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Honden of Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Honden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city 2.JPG|Honden of Sumiyoshi Jinja </gallery>
* Auxiliary Shrines * Stone monument for “Mimachi” (Perpetual Serpent Vigil) ** Mimachi is the festival day for Benzaiten on an auspicious “Kishi (tsuchinoto-mi)” day, venerating the serpent deity. The serpent, as the messenger of Benzaiten, protects silkworm cultivation by controlling mice that harm silkworms, thus revered as a guardian of sericulture. * Cat deity Stone Monument ** Like the Mimachi monument, the Cat deity stone monument venerates cats that control mice (harmful to silkworms). Cat deity monuments are rare nationwide but are notably found in Marumori Town. ** Additionally, within the precincts are various other stone monuments: Ashioyama stele, Aosa Daigongen (青麻大権現), Silk Moth Memorial Monument, Yama no Kami Monument, Yudonosan Stone Monument (for Yudono-san), Kinkazan Stone Monument (for Kinkasan), Amaterasu Kōtaijingū/Konpira Daigongen Stone Monument, Kofune Jinja, and Koyamimine Kami Stone Monument.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Stone shrines of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Various stone monuments at Sumiyoshi Jinja File:Mimachi-shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Mimachi stone monument File:Nekogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Cat deity stone monument </gallery>
* Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine (Silkworm deity Shrine) ** Originally enshrined in the Kasamatsu area and relocated to Sumiyoshi Jinja due to road expansion works. There are three small shrine buildings; the largest enshrines a wooden statue of a slender female deity in a red robe called “Kaiko-gami-sama (Oshira-kami-sama).” Kaiko-gami-sama reflects the history of sericulture in Edano area and is worshipped as a guardian of silkworms, protector against eye diseases, misfortune, and as a guardian deity for children.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Kaikogami shrine of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine File:Statue of Goddess of Silkworm of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Female deity statue of Kaiko-gami-sama Shrine </gallery>
* Sacred Trees ** Two ancient cedar trees about 700 years old stand along the approach. * Ema-den (Hall of Votive Tablets) ** The Ema-den was repurposed from the old haiden, originally rebuilt in Shōtoku 2, and used until Shōwa 3 (1928).
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Old Sugi trees of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.jpg|Ancient cedar trees (~700 years old) File:Emaden of Sumiyoshi-jinja shrine in Kakuda city.JPG|Ema-den of Sumiyoshi Jinja (former haiden) </gallery>
== References == * “Miyagi-ken Kakuda-shi Edano: Sumiyoshi Jinja Goyui-sho” (Sumiyoshi Jinja Shrine Office pamphlet)
== External Links == * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010234 Sumiyoshi Jinja] (Miyagi Prefecture Jinja-chō)

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft.
